source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.1 Generalities (pp. 28-31)

questions.q: Which file defines a Linux user by login name, UID, GID, command interpreter and home directory?
questions.why: /etc/passwd holds those fields. The encrypted password lives in /etc/shadow, and the prompt is set via /etc/profile.

questions.q: In the command prompt, what does a '#' indicate versus a '$'?
options: '#' is the administrator (root) prompt; '$' is an ordinary user
'#' is an ordinary user; '$' is the administrator
'#' means the shell is busy; '$' means it is ready
'#' is bash; '$' is the Korn shell
questions.why: A '#' prompt symbolises administrators, a '$' symbolises other users.

questions.q: Which shell is assigned by default to new users?
questions.why: bash is the most frequently used interpreter and the default for new users; ksh and csh are alternatives advanced users may choose.

questions.q: By convention, where are users' login (home) directories stored?
questions.why: Login directories are by convention under /home, and the login directory is the current directory at login.

questions.q: What is the difference between a TTY and a PTS terminal?
options: tty is a physical terminal; pts is a virtual console within a graphical environment
tty is virtual; pts is physical
tty is local; pts is always remote over SSH
They are two names for the same thing
questions.why: tty represents a physical terminal, pts a virtual console (with an instance number 0,1,2...) inside a graphical environment.

questions.q: Which key sequence interrupts a running command?
questions.why: Ctrl+C interrupts the command currently running in the shell.

questions.q: In the syntax 'command [option(s)] [argument(s)]', what do the square brackets indicate?
options: That the enclosed option or argument is optional
That the enclosed item is mandatory
That the item must be typed literally, including the brackets
That only one of the items may be chosen
questions.why: Square brackets mark an optional element; a vertical bar (pipe) inside them, like [a|e|i], separates mutually exclusive choices.

questions.q: How do short options differ from long options?
options: Short options begin with one dash (-l); long options begin with two dashes (--list)
Short options begin with two dashes; long options begin with one
Short options need no dash; long options need one
There is no syntactic difference, only length
questions.why: A single dash introduces short options, two dashes introduce long options, and a lone '--' marks the end of the option list.

questions.q: Which single command is equivalent to 'ls -l -i -a'?
questions.why: Several short options can be grouped behind one dash, so 'ls -l -i -a' is the same as 'ls -lia'.
